title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 06

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which the Civil War transformed life on the home front in the United States from 1861 to 1865.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Original simulated letter from a northern factory worker to his brother in the Union army, Lowell, Massachusetts, 1862; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Primary text source.
“The mill runs on army cloth, and the owners say the war has made business necessary to the nation. Wages have risen some, but prices rise faster. Some men enlist for bounty money, while others remain because mothers, wives, and children depend on factory pay. The war is far from the shop floor, yet it reaches every payday.”
Sourcing Cues: The letter reflects the perspective of a wage worker experiencing wartime production, inflation, and labor pressures.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Original simulated diary entry from a Confederate woman managing a small farm, Georgia, 1863; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Primary text source.
“With my husband away, I bargain for salt, mend harness, and plant what corn I can. The army takes horses and asks for bacon, but the children cannot eat promises. Neighbors speak of patriotism, yet many women ask how a country can be defended if its households are left without bread.”
Sourcing Cues: The diary reflects the burdens of war on Confederate women, rural households, and food supply.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Described map created for this practice exam, United States and Confederacy, 1861–1865.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows major Union railroad corridors connecting northeastern cities, the Midwest, and military supply centers. It also shows the Union naval blockade along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts, Confederate rail lines with fewer connections, and areas of major food shortages in parts of the Confederacy by 1864.
Sourcing Cues: The map emphasizes how transportation, blockade, and infrastructure shaped home-front experiences differently in the Union and Confederacy.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Original simulated testimony from an enslaved man who reached a Union army camp, coastal South Carolina, 1862; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Testimony-style excerpt.
“When the gunboats came near, people listened for news. Some left at night and brought children with them. The soldiers first called us contraband, but we knew we were not goods. Work in the camp was hard, yet it was work done with the hope that the old master’s claim was broken.”
Sourcing Cues: The testimony reflects the perspective of enslaved people using wartime disruption to seek freedom and reshape the meaning of the conflict.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Wartime price table created for this practice exam, Union and Confederacy, 1861–1865.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Home-Front Economic Pressures During the Civil War.”
Indicator | Union states | Confederate states
Industrial production | expanded in many areas | limited by blockade and shortages
Inflation | significant but manageable for many households | extremely severe by 1864–1865
Food supply | uneven but generally stronger | severe shortages in many regions
Government finance | taxes, bonds, and paper currency | paper currency and shortages of credit
Civilian unrest | draft and labor protests in some cities | food riots and desertion pressures in some areas
Sourcing Cues: The table compares economic stress across the Union and Confederacy and highlights uneven effects of wartime mobilization.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Original simulated broadside from a northern anti-draft meeting, New York City, 1863; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Political broadside excerpt.
“The rich man may purchase a substitute while the laborer is ordered from his family. If this war is for Union, let sacrifice be equal. If it is now also for emancipation, let politicians explain why workingmen must fight while their own sons remain at home.”
Sourcing Cues: The broadside reflects class resentment, racial politics, and opposition to conscription in some northern urban communities.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Original simulated circular from a freedpeople’s aid association, Boston, 1864; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Reform organization circular.
“Teachers, clothing, tools, and books are needed for people escaping slavery into Union lines. The war has opened a door, but freedom requires more than arrival. Schools, paid labor, family protection, and legal recognition must follow military victory.”
Sourcing Cues: The circular reflects northern reformers’ efforts to support formerly enslaved people and connect wartime emancipation to social reconstruction.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which the Civil War transformed home-front life from 1861 to 1865. The thesis must make an argument, such as that the war substantially transformed labor, gender roles, household economies, race relations, and federal or military involvement in civilian life, though effects differed sharply by region, race, and class.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as sectional conflict, secession, slavery, industrial differences between North and South, the Union blockade, mobilization for total war, emancipation, or prewar gender and labor systems.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about wartime industry, household labor, transportation and blockade, enslaved people’s flight to Union lines, inflation, conscription, class conflict, or aid to freedpeople.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Homestead Act, Morrill Land-Grant Act, Pacific Railway Act, Legal Tender Act, National Banking Acts, New York City Draft Riots, Emancipation Proclamation, United States Colored Troops, Sanitary Commission, Richmond Bread Riot, or Clara Barton.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze the northern worker’s class perspective, the Confederate woman’s household burden, the map’s emphasis on infrastructure, the formerly enslaved man’s view of freedom, the anti-draft broadside’s political purpose, or the aid circular’s reform goals.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as explaining that the Civil War transformed the home front unevenly; comparing Union and Confederate experiences; showing how emancipation changed the war’s meaning; or explaining that wartime hardship expanded some opportunities while increasing coercion, violence, and inequality for others.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that the Civil War transformed home-front life to a large extent by expanding wartime production, disrupting households, intensifying inflation and food shortages, creating class and draft conflict, and opening paths toward emancipation. It should also compare Union and Confederate experiences and explain how race, class, gender, and region shaped those changes. Useful document groupings include industrial labor, Confederate household hardship, infrastructure and blockade, enslaved people’s wartime agency, economic pressure, anti-draft politics, and aid to freedpeople. Relevant outside evidence could include the Emancipation Proclamation, New York City Draft Riots, Richmond Bread Riot, National Banking Acts, Legal Tender Act, U.S. Colored Troops, or the Sanitary Commission.
